item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After reading the update history this article has really come a long way, especially since it was at one point up for deletion. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a little shocked that this is only +20 after five years.

We start with a workman-like competent execution of mythology turned SCP. Nothing spectacular, but no serious flaws. The "Babel construction site" would be enough to secure a lukewarm upvote from me.

Then the letter kicks in.

A true man of God seeks out the ruins of humanity's greatest affront to heaven, rebuilds it better and stronger, defying a holy curse through pure monkey cleverness, and embarks to not just spit in God's eye, but to literally storm the gates of heaven. With atomic weapons. All to prove a technical point of theology and win a debate with his pen pal, or die trying.

The only way this could be more metal is if he rode the bomb in, Dr. Strangelove style.

But he's not an angry man, out to punish heaven for the injustice of the human condition. He's not a frothing lunatic corrupted by eldritch horrors. Grisham commits the ultimate blasphemy conceivable as a direct logical conclusion of his deeply held faith in the very deity he is trying to annihilate.

What I'm saying is: excellent nutshell characterization, an impeccable internal logic, and a dead-on nailing of the understated, detached tone. This deserves much more love than it's gotten over the years.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
God is dead, and Arthur Grisham killed him… the mad lad.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This seems like the premise is very similar to 1139. Is that intentional? Is there a connection in your mind, or should that be referenced in the skip?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Foundation found it rebuilt but empty. My intention when I wrote it was that Arthur had successfully rebuilt it and gone somewhere (maybe heaven maybe just a Way) with a nuke.

I hadn't thought of the preemptive smiting aspect, but that is a potentially interesting interpretation. It was not my original intent, I deliberately left the outcome of the experiment vague partially to reduce the number of people who were offended by it and partially because I had some ideas for a tale describing a much stranger outcome to the experiment than anything hinted at here.

However from Arthur's point of view preemptive smiting would have been a relatively positive conclusion to the experiment. It would have suggested that his god had at least some measure of power despite his doubts. (Remember Arthur wanted to this experiment to fail. He would rather not have spent most of his life serving something only to have it turn out to be little more than a collective hallucination. He is trying to prove that God is different from the other divinities he's encountered)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Did the Foundation acquire this with or without people inside?
It sounds to me like the Foundation found this thing empty but in perfect condition. Which also sounds to me like Arthur foolishly didn't expect god to preemptively smite him and his cohorts before they got a chance to even try it, which seems like the most obvious outcome for this type of experiment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm glad you like it. The teleportation effect was intended to bring the effect closer into line with the biblical descriptions of babel (the were made to speak different languages and scattered across the world). I stuck closer to the myth than I really needed to as I wildly underestimated how wildly known the story is.

Also if you like this concept of divinity checkout Terry Pratchet's small gods, or P.C. Hodgel's god stalk.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love the idea that faith is what empowers and even creates our "gods" and the fact that it is mostly unused in every form of media (Tyranny being a notable exception) saddens me. I say this is a great and clinical retelling of an ancient story known by all, the machinery that bypasses the language problem is just pure genious. The only thing I find lacking in this scip is the teleportation effect, it would be way better without it (Any people living near the tower would have to get accostumed to random desperate strangers speaking a tongue nobody can understand coming into town once in a while). I hope this gets the upvotes it deserves, it certainly has mine!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice to see someone catch the connection. There was a long section that explicitly referenced Nimrod in some of the early drafts, but it eventually got cut in an attempt to make the early part of the article flow better.

Although the idea of it being a continuation of an ancient plan by more advanced technological means plays nicely into the theme of technology and human ingenuity overcoming obstacles (translation computer, robotic trucks, etc…), so if I might bring some more of that back when I get around to rewriting this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry to see this SCP rated so low. I think it is fascinating! One reason is because of the association of Babel to the character of Nimrod. (Genesis 10:8-10) And according to Flavius Josephus in Jewish Antiquities, Nimrod "threatened to have his revenge on God if He wished to inundate the earth again; for he would build a tower higher than the water could reach and avenge the destruction of their forefathers."

So the idea that modern man was inadvertently carrying out, with nuclear fire, the last stage of a plan that began thousands of years ago with clay bricks… wow…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lapidate means to pelt with stones. I doubt that that is the word that you intended.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could use improving and rewriting. This concept is amazing, but dull and non-lapidated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But what would it be without the religious aspect? It's the tower of babel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like this would be better without the blatant religious aim. It's not bad, but its not super good either

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have the text saved in a couple of places. In terms of rewrites I could see doing Grisham's career as a tale. I just haven't seen a good way to fix this in SCP format (The tower is the skip but it is also the bit that doesn't seem to interest people as much, additionally the tower of babble is apparently widely enough known that references to it in a tale should be readily recognizable.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Meh.

You may try reducing the length of the letter, or putting it behind a fold. I'd suggest an extraordinarily clinical "Cannot find any empirical evidence of 'heaven' or other multidimensional access. Only trace radio-logical elements have been detected…"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, Palaois, you have the text of this saved in case it gets deleted before you can think of a rewrite, yeah?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The intent was that Arthur Grisham does sincerely believe. He's worried that he might be wrong hence the desire to test gods nature with a nuke but he does not actually want to succeed. He hopes that he fails and thereby strengthens the claim that his god is special.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For regular readers, sure, but more casual/less nuanced readers might react the way I did and say the idea is completely unoriginal before they even read the important part. I'm not saying this is without flaws; everything on this site is flawed, and pretty much everything in the negatives doubly so. I'm just not entirely sure what the primary flaw is here, and may have been projecting newbie-me's mistake onto others. Maybe I'm reading more into this than I should, but it's whatever. This will probably be gone soon anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Rating is currently above the deletion threshold.

It needs editing (some capitalization, a repeated phrase…)

Honestly, I like the concept but I'm not sure about the nuking heaven thing. Generally those that believe wouldn't want to, and if you DON'T believe you can't. Unless there is something that Arthur Grisham doesn't mention (like we're about to be invaded by Angels or something.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
there's intense symbolism involved that the majority of readers overlook/fail to see.

Not really. I think we generally get that; at least, many of us do. It's just that symbolism isn't enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Uuuuugh. I'm sorry to see this one go, but it's just not really good enough.

I like a lot of the detail and I like the idea of trying to nuke heaven. I'm sort of imagining this by way of SCP-093, and what I imagine is really cool. Imagine an exploration of an alien heaven? I dunno. There are a lot of places this could go, but the places it goes right now aren't enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not replying to the deletion vote because I don't want to try to rewrite this, but I'd like to rescind my previous comment "We already have one lol." That was dumb of me to say.

This has the same issue the DEER has, I think; there's intense symbolism involved that the majority of readers overlook/fail to see. I actually like it now that I see it from that perspective. However, I don't think changing my vote will be enough to save this. I honestly can't think of a way to improve on this without taking it in an entirely new direction. Author, if a writer more skilled than I can provide insight into rewriting and reposting this, I'd very much like to encourage you to do so. Perhaps focus on the same concept (nuking Heaven as a scientific test of one's faith) using another biblical or religious myth; a Tale detailing the same may also work.

Please, don't let the story here die.

item.title: Staff Post - Deletion Vote
item.content:encoded:
24 hours have elapsed since the posting of this article. Beginning deletion vote at -11.

If you want to ask for the opportunity to rewrite this article, you may reply to this post. Please do not reply to this post for any other reason if you are not staff.

Edit: Article is now above the deletion threshold. Deletion vote has been cancelled.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find the writing in this pretty clunky.

If this would mean that an individual would be unable to speak in any language, due to all the languages that they know already being spoken by someone within the effect of SCP-1643, they instead find themselves fluent in another language.

The device appears to have been trained by having individuals who enter the tower remember an extensive list of ordered terms and having them speak it into the central machine to calibrate it.

These are some examples of confusing and strangely worded sentences.

I also think the affect isn't very interesting, what with the languages. It seems literal for the tower of Babel.

Firing nukes at heaven is an interesting concept, but I think it can be successfully applied in a different article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is certainly not meant to be built by Horizon initiative. (although it is kind of cool to hear that something I wrote got linked on TVtopes, however incorrectly)

Looking back on this I think a good number of the problems with it stem from the fact was not originally meant to as a SCP to nuke heaven. The idea originally was an experiment in how advancing technology could be used to work around previously insurmountable barriers as applied to the tower of babel. I see now that I got rather bogged down in technical descriptions of various sorts that never succeeded in pulling together into a coherent theme/conveying emotional impact.

However writing this I realized that I needed a reason for someone to rebuild the tower and came up with the hole nuking heaven story. With a couple months perspective I can see that the second story really should have been the main focus of this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Generally speaking, this is rather bland. Not catchy, and the only reason I continued reading was simply out of pure boredom.

It could have a lot more "pizazz" than a partially made tower that teleports people to different parts of the world. Expand on that part.

I did, however, give an upvote simply because the idea of nuking heaven is hilarious. Reading about how the Foundation researchers ran tests and messed up the object would be even better, in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy shit! I didn't even realize! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found this via the TV Tropes page (which says this was built by the Horizon Initiative, by the way), and… I'm rather unimpressed. For an SCP meant to nuke Heaven, this is pretty much a straight-up retelling of the Tower of Babel with elements of Fallout. Freakin' Warehouse 13 did a more clever interpretation with this concept, and that was a cameo of the concept at best.

And do they know what this endeavor is worth? Surely they know that Heaven is a place on Earth.1

I hope this gets a worthy rewrite, though. It's not a bad concept, but the execution is lacking.

Footnotes
1. I had to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dijkstra

Never thought I'd see the old country spelling of my family name on this site.

Back to the scip, I find the idea quite interesting, but I can recognize some of the issues the others have pointed out. I don't think they detract too much. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The beginning is quite dull and didn't really intrigue me, the only reason I got to the end was reading some of the comments and seeing references to "nuking heaven". That said the later half did effectively draw me in and it had some interesting ideas. Overall I think it deserves a +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't like the execution, but I found the idea of a man nuking heaven a bit unique. After more consideration I have to down vote though.
The tower of babel might work with a different angle but nothing about this iteration really hooks me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The letter implies that this guy only expects nuking heaven to work if his assumption that faith created god is correct. As such the standard rules of the material world are not what he is depending on, he is using a nuclear weapon as a symbol of destruction (under the premise that something that people have a great deal of faith in the destructive potential of to attack something that he theorized is created solely do to people belief in it).

So from his perspective there are two possible outcomes:

1) Nuking heaven has no effect. His faith is confirmed and presumably god stops anything too bad from happening (except maybe to him but that is something he is willing to risk for answers). This is the outcome he would actually prefer.

2) Nuking heaven works-he proves his theory about the nature of god, but under this scenario god just one of many entities created by the faith invested in them and isn't particularly special.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvoted.

It's a pretty plain interpretation, and didn't really grab me at all. The bombing heaven part is incredibly, incredibly stupid. You'd be fighting things that don't operate by the standard rules of the material world, and would probably give off more radiation than the fallout.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was going to neutral vote for the twist, but on further reflection it didn't do much for me. I don't think that bombing heaven is actually that interesting. Without that twist, this is just the tower of babel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The really funny thing is that we have a third skip which is actually named the Tower of Babel on the main list.

(I'd also point out that while the linguistic effects ended up being similar, the articles do not have a lot else in common)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We already have a Tower of Babel. Just saying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm rather meh on this one. As others have said, it's a pretty straightforward SCPification of the Tower of Babel, which nearly had me downvoting right there. The twist at the end of the guy planning to nuke God was… okay, but not enough to bring this to an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ line-by-line
- Hide text

Local authorities are to be informed that the site is a government owned atmospheric research facility.

I'll go into some of my larger concerns later, but this seems extremely ill-considered. I mean, this is in Iraq, where the government isn't able to even effectively control large portions of the country, let alone build a gigantic "atmospheric research" facility.

Personnel assigned to the project should not be strongly religious. In particular personnel should not be practicing members of any Abrahamic faith.

You don't really explain why this is necessary.

Upon containment, ritual equipment consistent with that believed to be used to stabilize temporary inter-dimensional special anomalies was found within this chamber.

I think you mean "spatial" here, but if you don't, I have no idea what a "special anomaly" is beyond a redundant term. This also implies that "stabilizing interdimensional anomalies" is a concept that is rational and repeatable enough to be used as a baseline for comparison of other phenomena, which I'm not really a fan of here.

Within the base of the tower is a complex system of communication equipment and computers.

The rest of this paragraph is a little overlong. I personally don't really need to know the ins and outs of the translation device, and it makes the piece drag in my reading of it.

Several trucks with basic self-driving capabilities added to them

"added to them" seems like extraneous text to me in this sentence.

I didn't really care for this one. The front part of the article is a straight-up skipification of the Tower of Babel, which was pretty uninteresting to me and heavy on extraneous detail that made the narrative drag a bit. The back end is the twist that it's being rebuilt (itself kind of a tired trope) so that some guy can nuke God for some reason.

The piece is obviously written with a checklist of things in mind gleaned from general advice given on the site; existing stories aren't interesting unless there's some sort of twist involved, there has to be a narrative, clinical tone, etc. Without commenting on the soundness of these precepts, the way the writer goes about achieving these seems pretty clunky to me. The reason I should be interested in this is backloaded at the very end, which means you have an article where there's a not-very-good part that has to be saved by a better part, which isn't a very strong dynamic. Ideally, the document should be interesting throughout. One way to achieve that is to weave the elements of your story into the article in a way that's maybe not so clearly delineated as what's happening here.

Unlike a lot of other commenters, I'm not really that interested in the twist. Guy's going to use the Tower of Babel to blow up God. It seems…I dunno. Kind of angsty pseudo-Nietzchean. It doesn't help that this is also yet another "helpful note left behind by the now-vanished skipmaker", a device that's been beaten into the ground around here.

I'm also really bothered by the fact that apparently nobody noticed that someone was building a gigantic tower that teleports people. The containment section doesn't really seem to recognize that this would be extremely difficult to conceal from the public.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've made some edits aimed at improving the pacing and removing some extraneous material from the beginning of the article. Hopefully this helps.

I also tweaked the last bit of the letter to make the nuking Heaven bit a little harder to miss.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the central idea (as said above, nuking heaven…ha!).

The execution feels like slog to get there. Stripping out the fluff would go a long way: details such as the tapering walls and floor dimensions don't really move the story along. The fact that this is the Tower of Babel is made *readily* apparent in the first paragraph of the description.

No vote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Personally I like it, particularly because you never use the phrase "tower of babel": ie, its an ancient tower that does weird things to spacetime and language. There happens to be mention of something similar in the lore of the Abrahamic Faiths. Make of that what you will.

+ That said, suggestions for improvement:
- Just my thoughts

Very, very important: Pace your reveal, as per this excellent guide.

By putting the language and relocation bit right at the beginning, you show your monster, and you lose anyone who goes "Its just the Tower of Bable. I don't like it" before they even get to your real twist right at the end. Start off with current first sentance followed by the paragraphs describing the tower itself, between this line:

The lower 70 meters of the tower are…

and this line:

…Metallurgical analysis indicates that this section of SCP-1643 was built within the last decade.

Thats your first hook. Someone was trying to pick up where the ancients left off building this tower for some reason, and its got something to do with breaching spacetime. People will then stew on that even after they figure out its the Tower of Babel.

You might also consider redacting the the name of the town:

Based on translations of inscriptions in Akkadian, the town in which the tower was located was named ███████, which translates approximately to ‘Gate of God’.

Given that its implied that they don't want religious personnel deciding that this particular ancient abomination needs to be blown to bits, ergo, the restrictions against Abrahamic believers as staff for the site, this isn't exactly the sort of information you'd want to casually mention in the part of the article available to all personnel. Alternately, you could put this right at the end in a high-classification note. On the meta-level, while interesting trivia, its not worth showing your monster with the name "Bab-ilu". If you've already gotten it this can come across as a "do you get it yet?", and if you haven't, it potentially blows the fridge horror right there.

Lastly, I especially like the idea of using computers and drones to circumvent the effects. Some interesting experiments and questions come to mind:

  • What happens when someone actively or passively (re)learns that teir in German means door in English. Do they quickly forget the connection? Do they forget both languages and get issued a third language?
  • What would happen if you had a person who knew the computer's rosetta stone template, or programming languages enter the area? Does the computer count as speaking assembly code or java, but not the languages its translating blind-idiot style?
  • What would happen if you used a drone that responded to voice commands and could respond with prerecorded messages? Would it end up requesting "Por favor comando repetir", and get shipped to Guadalajara upon leaving the area?

Stuff like that might make an interesting side article- I'd keep it separate so you don't dilute the punch of that awesome twist at the end, but it could make for interesting padding.

Just some thoughts and suggestions, like I said.

Neutral vote for now, but verging on a +1 with some tinkering.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's okay but it didn't quite grab me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do agree that it drags at the beginning, but I thought the nuclear twist was well-executed enough to carry the rest of the article. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoted for being the Tower of Babel without a very interesting twist. Nuking heaven is an alright idea but was not nearly good enough to dissuade me from taking my mouse away from the downvote button, where it had been hanging since the very first paragraph.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nuking heaven.

Ha!

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Knew it was the tower of Babylon when it talked about people unable to talk in the same language.
Nicely done, I also like how people have dealt with the effects.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What I keep getting stuck on is that this isn't my idea of a scipified Tower of Babel, which is silly because this one is really pretty cool. I almost missed the bit at the end about atomic weapons, which would've been a shame because that's what secured my vote. Much better than when I first saw it, overall. Not perfect, but damn interesting enough in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy shit you're right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is what I thought until I realized that this guy is planning to nuke Heaven. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As discussed in chat, neutral voting. It gets good towards the end but the first bits are rather dull.
